_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the technical fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.cape: The Shiller CAPE ratio, or cyclically adjusted price-to-earnings ratio, divides the S&P 500 index price by the average real earnings over the last 10 years, smoothing earnings volatility to measure valuation extremes. At 42.18 in July 2026, this reading is more than double the 140-year historical average of 17.3, surpassed only once before during the December 1999 tech bubble peak at 44.19. Elevated CAPE levels have historically predicted subdued or negative forward returns; over past instances above 39, average returns were around -4% over one year and -20% over two years, reflecting pronounced downside risk. However, CAPE does not indicate when a correction will occur, as valuations can remain elevated for extended periods while liquidity and momentum sustain markets.
notes.buffett: The Buffett Indicator is the ratio of total US equity market capitalization to nominal GDP, serving as a broad valuation gauge by assessing stock prices relative to economic output. At 231.7% in June 2026, this metric significantly exceeds prior peaks of 146% during the dot-com bubble and 110% at the 2007 pre-crisis high, indicating extreme market overvaluation. Warren Buffett himself labeled readings above 200% as 'playing with fire,' highlighting increased vulnerability to correction as prices diverge from economic fundamentals. Nonetheless, like CAPE, elevated Buffett Indicator levels do not provide timing cues, and market capitalization can grow faster than GDP for structural or cyclical reasons temporarily.
notes.concentration: Market concentration metrics capture the portfolio weight of top stocks within an index, indicating risk of idiosyncratic shocks and liquidity bottlenecks. In 2026, the top 5 S&P 500 constituents account for nearly 29.8% of the index, with the technology sector comprising an outsized 48%, and the top 10 stocks weighting 41%, up sharply from 17.5% in 2015. Such concentration increases systemic exposure to a handful of large-cap tech firms, amplifying volatility if these leaders falter and potentially challenging market breadth during a downturn. However, concentration alone is not a direct valuation measure and may reflect sectoral growth dynamics or index construction nuances.
notes.yieldcurve: The yield curve spread between 10-year Treasury yields and 3-month T-bill rates is a commonly used monetary signal; here it stands at 0.71 percentage points in July 2026. A positive but modestly steep curve indicates expectations of economic growth and moderate inflation, while flatter or inverted curves have historically forecast recessions. The New York Fed's recession probability metric currently at 31% conditional on this spread suggests elevated but not imminent recession risk, reflecting uncertainty about economic trajectory. Yet, yield curve signals can be influenced by global capital flows and central bank policies, limiting precise timing for market reversals solely on this basis.
notes.drypowder: Dry powder refers to the cash reserves available for investment, often held by large institutions or conglomerates ready to deploy capital opportunistically. Berkshire Hathaway's cash hoard reached a record $397.4 billion in Q1 2026, indicating substantial latent buying power potentially awaiting market dislocations. Despite this abundance, 79% of institutions expect their cash levels to decline over the near term, suggesting optimism or pressure to remain invested. While large cash reserves can provide liquidity support at market inflection points, the timing and decisiveness of deployment are uncertain and subject to market sentiment shifts.
notes.finale: Valuation extremes like historically high CAPE and Buffett Indicator readings set the magnitude of potential market declines but do not predict the timing of when liquidity constraints—akin to music stopping in musical chairs—will trigger a sell-off. The imminent window from December 2026 to May 2027, marked by IPO lock-up expirations for firms like SpaceX, OpenAI, and Anthropic, presents a plausible liquidity shock as insiders gain the ability to sell shares. Such events can increase market supply abruptly, straining liquidity and forcing repricing in concentrated sectors. Thus, while elevated valuations indicate vulnerability, the confluence of structural concentration, significant cash reserves, and looming lock-up expirations composes a complex, uncertain environment where timing remains challenging but systemic liquidity shocks are credible triggers.
